title: Gilbertiodendron ivorense
intro: Gilbertiodendron ivorense, also known as African butter tree, is a plant species native to West Africa, characterized by its large, deciduous leaves and edible seeds. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its various health benefits, including the treatment of fever, cough, and respiratory infections, as well as its anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. The bioactive constituents of Gilbertiodendron ivorense include flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made from the plant are tea, infusion, and paste, which are used to alleviate respiratory issues and promote wound healing.
taxonomy: Gilbertiodendron ivorense is classified in the family Fabaceae, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants commonly known as legumes. This family is further divided into several subfamilies, including the Caesalpinioideae subfamily to which Gilbertiodendron ivorense belongs. The Caesalpinioideae subfamily is characterized by its unique flower structure and is found in tropical and subtropical regions around the world. At the genus level, Gilbertiodendron ivorense is part of the Gilbertiodendron genus, which is a group of trees native to tropical Africa. The species is also sometimes referred to by its older name, Afzelia ivorense.
